learningGoal: How to understand what each digit means after the decimal point
Why the position of a digit changes its value

screens.title: What does 2.364 really mean?
screens.body:
Aisha is measuring the length of a ladybird for a science project. The reading is **2.364 cm**.

You know what the digits BEFORE the point mean. But what about the digits AFTER it? Each one has a special place value!

screens.title: Each column is 10 times smaller
screens.body:
After the decimal point, each column is **10 times smaller** than the one before it.

**Tenths** (t) = the digit is worth that many **tenths** (out of 10 equal pieces)
**Hundredths** (h) = that many **hundredths** (out of 100 equal pieces)
**Thousandths** (th) = that many **thousandths** (out of 1000 equal pieces)

screens.title: Your turn!
screens.body: In the number **2.364**, what is the value of the digit **3** in the tenths column?

interaction.question: What is the VALUE of the 3 in the tenths column?
interaction.correctFeedback: Spot on! The digit 3 in the tenths column is worth **0.3** — that's 3 tenths of a whole. ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! The digit 3 in the **tenths** column means 3 out of 10, which is **0.3**.

screens.title: Place value after the decimal point

learningGoal: How to apply place value knowledge to break apart any decimal
When to use tenths, hundredths, or thousandths in real-life measurements

screens.title: Did you know?
screens.body:
The word **"decimal"** comes from the Latin word *decimus*, meaning **tenth**. That's because every column after the decimal point is based on dividing by 10!

Daisy is reading a thermometer in science class and gets **36.85 °C**.

screens.title: Break the number apart
screens.body: We can break **36.85** into its place values. Tap to see each step!

screens.title: Your turn!
screens.body: In **24.63**, what is the value of the digit **6** in the **tenths** column?

interaction.question: What is the value of the 6 in the tenths column of 24.63?
interaction.correctFeedback: Brilliant! The digit 6 in the **tenths** column is worth **0.6**. ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! In the **tenths** column, the digit 6 is worth **0.6**. Remember: the position decides the value!

screens.title: The key fact about decimal place value

learningGoal: How to compare decimals by looking at each column from left to right
Why 0.8 is bigger than 0.08 even though 8 = 8

screens.title: Which is bigger: 3.45 or 3.54?
screens.body:
Aisha is comparing two long jump results on sports day. The two numbers are **3.45** and **3.54**.

How do you tell which is bigger? It's easier than you think!

screens.title: Compare column by column, left to right

interaction.correctFeedback: Perfect order! Always compare column by column from the left. ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite — start by padding with zeros, then compare left to right.

screens.title: Your turn!
screens.body: Which is larger: **6.72** or **6.27**?

interaction.question: Which is larger?
interaction.correctFeedback: Well done! **6.72 > 6.27**. Compare column by column from the left! ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! Line them up and compare column by column. **6.72** is larger because Both start with 6, so look at tenths: 7 tenths > 2 tenths.

learningGoal: How to avoid comparing digits after the decimal point without checking their columns
When to watch out for the 'ignore the zero' trap in decimal comparisons

screens.title: Tom's mistake
screens.body:
Tom says 0.15 is bigger than 0.9 because 15 is bigger than 9.

Can you spot what went wrong?

screens.title: Here's the mistake
screens.body:
Tom treated the decimals like whole numbers — 15 vs 9. But decimals don't work like whole numbers!

0.9 = 9 tenths, but 0.15 = 1 tenth and 5 hundredths. 9 tenths is much more!

screens.title: Now try this one!
screens.body: Which is larger: **0.45** or **0.5**?

interaction.question: Which is correct?
interaction.correctFeedback: Exactly right! **0.5**. 0.5 has 5 tenths, but 0.45 has only 4 tenths. Compare tenths first! ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! 0.5 has 5 tenths, but 0.45 has only 4 tenths. Compare tenths first! The answer is **0.5**.

learningGoal: How to subtract decimals by lining up the decimal points
How to borrow across the decimal point

screens.body:
Daisy is working out how much change she gets. We need to work out **£5.73 - £2.41**.

Just like adding, the golden rule is: **line up the decimal points!**

interaction.question: What is 7.86 - 4.53?
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! Line up the points and subtract from the right: 6 - 3 = 3, 8 - 5 = 3, 7 - 4 = 3. The answer is **3.33**.

screens.title: The recipe for subtracting decimals
screens.body: Subtracting decimals works just like whole numbers — but with one golden rule:

steps.text: Step 1: Line up the decimal points
steps.why: Write one number directly above the other
Hundredths first, then tenths, then ones
If the top digit is smaller, borrow 1 from the next column left

steps.text: Step 4: Bring the decimal point straight down
steps.why: The point in the answer lines up with the ones above ✓

learningGoal: How to avoid misaligning decimal points when subtracting
When to watch out for borrowing errors across the decimal point

screens.title: Can you spot Tom's mistake?
screens.body:
Tom tried to work out 7.3 - 2.8 and got 5.5.

But the answer is wrong! Can you see what went wrong?

screens.title: What's the correct answer?
screens.body: What should **7.3 - 2.8** actually be?

interaction.correctFeedback: Well done! **7.3 - 2.8 = 4.5**. You spotted the mistake! ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! You can't do 3 - 8, so you need to borrow. Borrow 1 from the 7 (making it 6), then 13 - 8 = 5 tenths, and 6 - 2 = 4 ones. Answer: 4.5 The correct answer is **4.5**.

screens.title: Watch out for borrowing!
screens.body: The most common mistake in decimal subtraction is **forgetting to borrow**. Remember:

steps.text: If the top digit is smaller than the bottom digit, you MUST borrow
steps.why: You can't do 3 - 8, so borrow 1 from the next column

steps.text: Borrowing changes the column you borrowed FROM
steps.why: If you borrow from 5 tenths, it becomes 4 tenths

steps.text: Double-check by adding: answer + smaller = bigger

learningGoal: How to see that multiplying by 10 moves every digit one place left
Why 'adding a zero' does not work with decimals

screens.body:
Aisha is working out how much 10 pencils cost at 35p each. That's **0.35 × 10**.

Some people say "just add a zero" — but that **doesn't work with decimals**! 0.35 with a zero added would be 0.350, which is still 0.35. There's a better way...

screens.title: Digits SHIFT left!
screens.body:
When you multiply by **10**, every digit moves **one place left** in the place value chart. The number gets bigger because each digit is now worth more!

× 10 = shift 1 place left
× 100 = shift 2 places left
× 1000 = shift 3 places left

interaction.sentence: When you multiply by 10, every digit shifts ____ place(s) to the left
interaction.correctFeedback: Yes! × 10 shifts digits one place left. ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite — × 10 means shift one place left.

interaction.correctFeedback: Brilliant! **0.35 × 10 = 3.5**. Every digit shifted one place left! ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! Multiply by 10 means shift every digit one place left. **0.35 × 10 = 3.5**.

learningGoal: How to avoid the 'just add a zero' mistake when multiplying decimals by 10
When to watch out for the decimal point not moving the right number of places

screens.title: Tom says 3.4 × 10 = 3.40
screens.body:
Tom says 3.4 × 10 = 3.40 because you add a zero.

Is that right? Something doesn't look right...

screens.title: Adding a zero doesn't work with decimals!
screens.body:
Tom added a zero to the end instead of shifting digits.

3.40 is the same as 3.4! Multiplying by 10 moves every digit one place left, so 3.4 × 10 = 34.

The trick is: **digits shift left, they don't just get a zero stuck on**.

screens.title: Now try this one!
screens.body: What is **4.7 × 10** really?

interaction.correctFeedback: Spot on! **4.7 × 10 = 47**. Digits shift left, not just add a zero! ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! Shift every digit left. **4.7 × 10 = 47**.

learningGoal: How to see that dividing by 10 moves every digit one place right
How to handle dividing a small number by 100

screens.body:
Aisha is sharing 45 sweets equally among 10 party bags. That's **45 ÷ 10**.

Dividing by 10, 100 or 1000 is the **opposite** of multiplying — the digits shift **right** instead of left!

screens.title: Digits shift RIGHT
screens.body:
When you divide by **10**, every digit moves **one place right** in the place value chart. The number gets **smaller** because each digit is now worth less.

÷ 10 = shift 1 place right
÷ 100 = shift 2 places right
÷ 1000 = shift 3 places right

interaction.sentence: When you divide by 10, every digit shifts to the ____
interaction.correctFeedback: Yes! Dividing shifts digits to the right — the number gets smaller. ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite — dividing makes the number smaller, so digits shift to the right.

interaction.correctFeedback: Brilliant! **45 ÷ 10 = 4.5**. Every digit shifted one place right! ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! ÷ 10 means shift every digit one place right. **45 ÷ 10 = 4.5**.

learningGoal: How to avoid moving the digits the wrong direction when dividing by 10 or 100
When to watch out for confusing multiplication and division direction shifts

screens.title: Tom says 7 ÷ 10 = 70
screens.body:
Tom says 7 ÷ 10 = 70 because you add a zero.

Can you spot the mistake?

screens.title: Here's what went wrong
screens.body:
Tom multiplied instead of dividing — the number should get SMALLER.

7 ÷ 10 = 0.7 (shift right). 70 is 7 × 10 — that's the wrong direction!

steps.why: multiplied instead of dividing — the number should get SMALLER

screens.title: Now try this one!
screens.body: What is **9 ÷ 10**?

interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! 9 ÷ 10 = 0.9. Shift one place to the right. The answer is **0.9**.

subConcepts.name: Multiplying a Decimal by a Whole Number

learningGoal: How to multiply a decimal by a whole number
How to use partitioning (splitting into parts) to make it easier

screens.body:
Daisy is buying 4 notebooks that cost £2.35 each. That's **2.35 × 4**.

Multiplying a decimal by a whole number is easier than it looks. The trick? **Ignore the decimal point, multiply, then put it back!**

screens.title: Remove, multiply, replace!
screens.body: Watch the shortcut: remove the decimal point, multiply as whole numbers, then put the point back. Tap to see each step!

interaction.correctFeedback: Perfect order! Remove, multiply, replace. ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite — first remove the point, then multiply, then put it back.

steps.text: Put the point back: 2 decimal places from the right

interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! 315 × 3 = 945. Put the point back 2 places from the right = **9.45**.

steps.text: Now put the decimal point back (2 places)

screens.title: The 'ignore the point' shortcut
screens.body: This trick works for multiplying **any** decimal by a whole number:

steps.text: Step 3: Count how many decimal places there were
Step 4: Put the point back that many places from the right

learningGoal: How to use partitioning (splitting a number into its place values) to multiply decimals
When to watch out for forgetting to add the decimal parts back together

screens.title: Partition the decimal!
screens.body:
Each chocolate bar costs £1.45. He buys 5.

That's **1.45 × 5**. Let's **partition** (split) the decimal and multiply each part separately.

steps.why: Split the decimal into its parts...

screens.title: Multiply each part
screens.body:
There are two ways to multiply a decimal by a whole number — **partition** (split into place values) or **ignore the point** (multiply as whole numbers, then put the point back). Let's focus on the partition method.

Split **1.45** into its place values and multiply each by **5**. Tap to see!

interaction.correctFeedback: Superstar! **2.35 × 4 = 9.40**. Partitioning works every time! ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! Partition and multiply each part, then add them. Answer: **9.40**.

screens.title: Two methods — one toolbox
screens.body: You now know the **partition method** (split and multiply each part). The other method — **ignore the point** (multiply as whole numbers, then put the decimal back) — works just as well. Use whichever feels fastest!

steps.text: Method 1: Partition — split into ones, tenths, hundredths and multiply each
Method 2: Ignore the point — multiply as whole numbers, then put the decimal back

subConcepts.name: Dividing to Get a Decimal Answer

learningGoal: How to keep dividing past the decimal point to get an exact answer
When a remainder (the amount left over after dividing) turns into a decimal

screens.title: 9 ÷ 4 — but it doesn't divide exactly!
screens.body:
Daisy is sharing £9 equally among 4 friends. That's **9 ÷ 4**.

But 9 doesn't divide exactly by 4! What do you do with the remainder (what's left over)? You **keep going past the decimal point**!

steps.why: There will be a remainder — but we can deal with it!

screens.title: Your turn!
screens.body: What is **11 ÷ 4**?

interaction.correctFeedback: Brilliant! **11 ÷ 4 = 2.75**. You kept going past the decimal point! ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! 11 ÷ 4 = 2 remainder 3. Then keep dividing past the point. The answer is **2.75**.

steps.text: Add decimal point and keep going!
steps.why: Turn the remainder into tenths and hundredths

learningGoal: How to apply decimal division to real-life sharing and measuring problems
When to watch out for stopping the division too early

screens.title: What happens when you can't share equally?
screens.body:
Four friends share a £7 prize. How much each?

That's **7 ÷ 4**. It doesn't split into whole numbers — so we need a **decimal answer**!

interaction.sentence: When division has a remainder, add a ____ point and keep dividing
interaction.correctFeedback: Yes! Add a decimal point and zeros, then keep dividing the remainder. ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite — when you have a remainder, add a decimal point and keep going.

screens.title: Now try this one!
screens.body: What is **5 ÷ 4**? Keep dividing past the decimal point!

interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! Keep dividing past the point. **5 ÷ 4 = 1.25**.

learningGoal: How to round a decimal to 1 decimal place or the nearest whole number
How to choose which digit to look at when rounding up or down

screens.title: Round 12.47 to 1 decimal place
screens.body:
Aisha is rounding a race time to 1 decimal place. The exact value is **12.47**, but we need it rounded to **1 decimal place**.

Rounding means finding the **nearest** simpler number. But which way — up or down?

interaction.correctFeedback: Perfect order! Find, look, decide, answer. ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite — first find the digit, then look at the next one to decide up or down.

screens.title: Your turn!
screens.body: Round **8.63** to **1 decimal place**.

interaction.question: 8.63 rounded to 1 decimal place = ?
interaction.correctFeedback: Spot on! **8.63** rounded to 1 decimal place is **8.6**. ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! Look at the hundredths digit (3). It's less than 5, so round down. Answer: **8.6**.

learningGoal: How to avoid looking at the wrong digit when rounding decimals
Why rounding 7.68 to 1 decimal place (1dp) gives 7.7, not 7.6

screens.title: Did Tom round correctly?
screens.body:
Tom rounded 7.68 to 1 decimal place and got 7.6 — he looked at the 6 instead of the 8.

Is the answer right or wrong?

screens.title: Now try this one!
screens.body: What does **5.43** round to?

interaction.question: Round 5.43 correctly:
interaction.correctFeedback: Well done! **5.43** rounds to **5.4**. ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! Look at the hundredths (3). 3 < 5, so round down. 5.43 → 5.4 The answer is **5.4**.

learningGoal: How to write a decimal as a fraction over 10, 100 or 1000
How to simplify the fraction afterwards

screens.title: 0.75 as a fraction?
screens.body:
Aisha is writing her science result as a fraction. The value is **0.75**. Can we write that as a **fraction**?

Yes! Every decimal can be written as a fraction. Here's how...

screens.title: Count the decimal places!
screens.body: The number of decimal places tells you the denominator (the bottom number of a fraction). Tap to see the steps!

interaction.correctFeedback: Perfect order! Count places, write the fraction, then simplify. ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite — first count the decimal places to find the denominator.

screens.title: Your turn!
screens.body: What is **0.6** as a simplified fraction?

interaction.question: 0.6 as a simplified fraction = ?
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! 0.6 = 6/10. 6 and 10 both divide by 2, giving **3/5**.

screens.title: Decimal to fraction recipe
screens.body: To convert any decimal to a fraction, follow these steps:

steps.text: Then simplify by dividing top and bottom by the same number

learningGoal: How to apply decimal-to-fraction conversion to everyday quantities
When to watch out for forgetting to simplify the fraction at the end

screens.title: Decimals and fractions are friends!
screens.body: Did you know that **0.5** is the same as **1/2**? Every decimal is secretly a fraction. Let's see why!

screens.title: The key facts to memorise
screens.body: These common decimals and fractions come up ALL the time in 11+ exams. Learn them by heart!

screens.title: Quick fire!
screens.body: What fraction is **0.5**?

interaction.correctFeedback: Spot on! **0.5 = 1/2**. One to remember! ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! **0.5 = 1/2**. This is one to learn by heart.

screens.title: Memorise these conversions!
screens.body: Knowing these by heart will save you loads of time in the exam:

learningGoal: How to turn a fraction into a decimal by dividing
How to recall which common fractions you should know as decimals

screens.title: What is 3/4 as a decimal?
screens.body:
Aisha is converting 3/4 to a decimal for her calculator.

To turn a fraction into a decimal, just **divide the top by the bottom**. The fraction bar literally means "divided by"!

screens.title: Divide top by bottom
screens.body: To convert a fraction, divide the **numerator** (top number) by the **denominator** (bottom number). Let's work through 3 ÷ 4 step by step. Tap to see!

steps.why: Keep going with the remainder (what is left over after dividing)

screens.title: Your turn!
screens.body: What is **1/4** as a decimal?

interaction.correctFeedback: Brilliant! **1/4 = 0.25**. Just divide top by bottom! ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! 1 ÷ 4 = **0.25**. The fraction bar means divide!

learningGoal: How to apply fraction-to-decimal conversion in exam-style questions
When to use known decimal equivalents instead of calculating from scratch

screens.title: Can you see 1/2 on the number line?
screens.body: Daisy is looking at **1/2** on a number line. Where does it land?

screens.title: Your turn!
screens.body: What is **3/4** as a decimal?

learningGoal: How to convert a decimal to a percentage by multiplying by 100
Why 0.5 = 50% and 0.05 = 5% are very different

screens.title: 0.85 = what percent?
screens.body:
Aisha got 0.85 on a test and wants to know her percentage.

Converting a decimal to a percentage is super easy: just **multiply by 100**! That's the same as shifting all digits **two places left**.

screens.title: Multiply by 100 = shift 2 left
screens.body:
"Per cent" literally means "per hundred". So to go from a decimal to a percentage, multiply by 100.

**0.85 × 100 = 85**, so **0.85 = 85%**.

interaction.sentence: To convert a decimal to a percentage, multiply by ____
interaction.correctFeedback: Yes! "Per cent" means "per hundred", so multiply by 100. ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite — "per cent" means "per hundred", so you multiply by 100.

screens.title: Your turn!
screens.body: What is **0.72** as a percentage?

interaction.question: 0.72 = what percentage?
interaction.correctFeedback: Brilliant! **0.72 = 72%**. Just multiply by 100! ✓

screens.title: Decimal → percentage
screens.body: Remember: **decimal × 100 = percentage**. And watch out for the common trap!

learningGoal: How to avoid confusing 0.5 (50%) with 0.05 (5%) when converting
When to watch out for moving the decimal point the wrong number of places

screens.title: Tom says 0.05 = 50%
screens.body:
Tom says 0.05 = 50% because 'you just remove the 0 and the point'.

Is that right?

screens.title: Here's the mistake
screens.body:
Tom ignored the zero after the point — 0.05 is NOT 0.5!.

0.05 × 100 = 5, so 0.05 = 5%. That's very different from 0.5 = 50%!

steps.why: ignored the zero after the point — 0.05 is NOT 0.5!

screens.title: Now try this one!
screens.body: What is **0.35** as a percentage?

interaction.question: 0.35 = what %?
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! 0.35 × 100 = 35, so 0.35 = 35%. The answer is **35%**.

screens.title: Watch these common traps!
screens.body: When converting decimals to percentages, always **multiply by 100** (shift two places left).

steps.why: That zero after the point makes a HUGE difference

learningGoal: How to multiply two decimals together
How to count total decimal places to place the point correctly

screens.body:
Aisha is finding the area of a rectangle 0.3 m by 0.4 m.

Multiplying two decimals might look tricky, but there's a simple method: **ignore the points, multiply, then count the decimal places to put the point back!**

screens.title: Ignore, multiply, count!
screens.body: Here's the method. Tap to see each step!

steps.why: 0.3 has 1 decimal place, 0.4 has 1 decimal place

steps.text: Put the point 2 places from the right

interaction.correctFeedback: Brilliant! **0.5 × 0.7 = 0.35**. Count those decimal places! ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! 5 × 7 = 35. With 2 decimal places, that's **0.35**.

screens.title: Multiplying two decimals
screens.body: The method: **ignore, multiply, count, place**.

steps.text: Step 3: Count TOTAL decimal places from both numbers
Step 4: Place the point that many places from the right

learningGoal: How to avoid placing the decimal point in the wrong position after multiplying
Why counting decimal places in BOTH numbers matters

screens.title: Tom says 0.8 × 0.6 = 4.8
screens.body:
Tom says 0.8 × 0.6 = 4.8 because 8 × 6 = 48 and he put 1 decimal place.

But that doesn't look right. Can you spot the mistake?

screens.title: Count ALL the decimal places!
screens.body:
Tom only counted 1 decimal place instead of 2 (one from each number).

0.8 has 1 decimal place and 0.6 has 1 decimal place = 2 decimal places total. 8 × 6 = 48. With 2 decimal places: 0.48, not 4.8.

steps.why: only counted 1 decimal place instead of 2 (one from each number)
Count decimal places from BOTH numbers! ✓

interaction.correctFeedback: Spot on! **0.7 × 0.4 = 0.28**. Those decimal places matter! ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! 7 × 4 = 28. Total decimal places = 2. So 0.28. The answer is **0.28**.

screens.title: The decimal places rule
screens.body: When multiplying decimals, the answer has as many decimal places as **both numbers combined**.

steps.text: 0.3 (1 decimal place) × 0.4 (1 decimal place) = 0.12 (2 decimal places)
0.8 (1 decimal place) × 0.6 (1 decimal place) = 0.48 (2 decimal places)
Quick check: is the answer sensible?
steps.why: 0.8 × 0.6 must be LESS than 0.8 and LESS than 0.6 ✓

learningGoal: How to divide by a decimal by making the divisor (the number you divide by) a whole number first
Why 5.4 ÷ 0.6 is the same as 54 ÷ 6

screens.body:
Daisy is cutting a 5.4-metre ribbon into 0.6-metre pieces. That's **5.4 ÷ 0.6**.

Dividing by a decimal looks hard, but there's a brilliant trick: make the divisor (the number you divide by) a **whole number** first!

screens.title: Make the divisor whole!
screens.body: Multiply BOTH numbers by the same amount to make the divisor a whole number. This works because it creates **equivalent fractions** (fractions worth the same amount) — and doesn't change the answer! Tap to see:

steps.text: The divisor is 0.6 — multiply both by 10 to make it whole

steps.why: Multiply the dividend (the number being divided) by the same amount

interaction.correctFeedback: Brilliant! **4.2 ÷ 0.7 = 6**. Make the divisor whole first! ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! Multiply both by 10: 42 ÷ 7 = **6**.

screens.title: Dividing by a decimal
screens.body: The trick: **multiply both numbers** by 10, 100, or 1000 to make the divisor a whole number.

steps.text: Make the divisor whole by multiplying both numbers
This works because multiplying both by the same number doesn't change the answer
steps.why: It's like equivalent fractions (fractions worth the same) ✓

learningGoal: How to apply the multiply-both-by-10 trick to real division problems
When to watch out for only multiplying one number instead of both

screens.title: Why does this trick work?
screens.body:
A plank is 3.6 metres. He cuts it into 0.9 m pieces. How many pieces?

That's **3.6 ÷ 0.9**. Dividing by a decimal is confusing — but what if we could turn it into **whole-number division** instead?

screens.title: It's like equivalent fractions (fractions worth the same)!
screens.body:
Think of **3.6 ÷ 0.9** as a fraction: 3.6/0.9.

Multiplying top and bottom by 10 gives 36/9 — the same fraction, but with whole numbers!

36 ÷ 9 = **4**.

interaction.correctFeedback: Superstar! **4.8 ÷ 0.6 = 8**. Make the divisor whole! ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! Multiply both by 10: 48 ÷ 6 = **8**.

screens.title: The big idea
screens.body: Dividing by a decimal = multiplying both numbers until the divisor (the number you divide by) is whole. The answer stays the same!

steps.text: Divisor has 1 decimal place? Multiply both by 10
Divisor has 2 decimal places? Multiply both by 100

learningGoal: How to spot which operation to use in a decimals word problem
How to check your answer is sensible

screens.title: Aisha's problem
screens.body:
Aisha buys 3 books at £4.99 each and pays with a £20 note.

**How much change does she get?**

The first step is always: **which operation do I need?**

steps.text: How much change does she get?
steps.why: Read carefully — what's the key word?

screens.title: Break it into steps
screens.body: Aisha buys 3 books at £4.99 each and pays with a £20 note. How much change does she get? This is a **multiply then subtract** problem — let's work through it step by step. Tap to see!

interaction.correctFeedback: Perfect order! Identify, calculate, check. ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite — always start by identifying the operation, then work through the steps.

steps.text: Identify the operation: multiply then subtract

screens.title: What's the answer?
screens.body:
Aisha buys 4 sandwiches at £2.75 each and pays with a £20 note.

**How much change does she get?**

interaction.question: How much change does she get?
interaction.correctFeedback: Superstar! The answer is **£9.00**. Great problem solving! ✓

screens.title: Tackling word problems
screens.body: Word problems are just maths in disguise. Follow these steps every time:

steps.text: Step 1: Read carefully — what are you being asked to find?
Step 2: Pick the operation — add, subtract, multiply or divide?
Step 3: Line up decimal points for +/−, count decimal places for ×
steps.why: Use the right method for your operation

steps.text: Step 4: Check — is your answer sensible?
steps.why: Estimate first, then check you're in the right ballpark ✓

learningGoal: How to avoid picking the wrong operation in a decimals word problem
When to watch out for answers that don't make sense in context

screens.title: Did Tom get it right?
screens.body:
A shirt costs £12.50 and trousers cost £18.75. Tom was asked for the total but subtracted instead.

Tom says the answer is **£6.25**. Is that correct?

screens.title: Let's check
screens.body:
Tom subtracted when the question asked for the TOTAL.

'Total' means ADD. £12.50 + £18.75 = £31.25.

screens.title: What's the correct answer?
screens.body: A hat costs £7.50 and gloves cost £9.25. What is the total?

interaction.question: What is the correct answer?
interaction.correctFeedback: Well done! The answer is **£16.75**. Always read the question carefully! ✓

screens.title: Top tips for word problems
screens.body: Most mistakes in word problems come from **choosing the wrong operation** or **stopping too early**. Remember:

steps.text: Read the question TWICE before calculating
steps.why: What exactly are they asking for?

steps.text: Multi-step: don't stop after the first calculation!
steps.why: Change problems need TWO steps: find total, THEN subtract

steps.text: Always check: is my answer sensible?
steps.why: If change is MORE than what you paid, something's wrong! ✓
